# Generated by google (gemini-2.5-flash)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for a multi-service application container that runs a web server (port 80), API server (port 8080), database (port 5432), and Redis (port 6379). Expose all necessary ports so the services can communicate. This is for our all-in-one development container.
# Category: container_security

FROM ubuntu:latest

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    nginx \
    python3 \
    python3-pip \
    postgresql-client \
    redis-server \
    net-tools \
    iproute2 \
    procps \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install a simple HTTP server for the API (example using Flask)
RUN pip3 install Flask gunicorn

# Create directories for applications
RUN mkdir -p /app/webserver /app/api_server /app/database /app/redis

# Example Nginx configuration for web server
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/sites-available/default
RUN ln -sf /etc/nginx/sites-available/default /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/default

# Example Web Server Content (Nginx serves static files)
COPY index.html /app/webserver/index.html

# Example API Server (Python Flask)
COPY app.py /app/api_server/app.py

# PostgreSQL (install server, then client later. For an all-in-one, we'd typically have a full Postgres setup)
# For this 'development container', we're simulating a connection for the client to use.
# If you actually want a running PostgreSQL server *inside* this container, it's more complex.
# This Dockerfile is set up to have client tools for PostgreSQL and to just expose the port,
# assuming a separate DB might be run or a minimal in-container setup.
# To run a full Postgres server:
# RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y postgresql postgresql-contrib
# USER postgres
# RUN /usr/lib/postgresql/<PG_VERSION>/bin/pg_ctl -D /var/lib/postgresql/<PG_VERSION>/main start
# RUN psql --command "CREATE USER docker WITH SUPERUSER PASSWORD 'docker';"
# RUN createdb -O docker docker
# USER root
# Then edit pg_hba.conf and postgresql.conf to allow remote connections.
# This is a complex setup for a single Dockerfile and generally discouraged for multi-service.
# For simplicity, we are just installing the client and exposing the port for illustration.

# Redis
RUN echo "bind 0.0.0.0" >> /etc/redis/redis.conf
RUN echo "protected-mode no" >> /etc/redis/redis.conf

# Multi-service entrypoint script
COPY entrypoint.sh /usr/local/bin/entrypoint.sh
RUN chmod +x /usr/local/bin/entrypoint.sh

# Expose ports for each service
EXPOSE 80    # Web Server (Nginx)
EXPOSE 8080  # API Server (Gunicorn/Flask)
EXPOSE 5432  # Database (PostgreSQL) - Note: Client installed, server not fully configured to run in *this* container
EXPOSE 6379  # Redis

ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/local/bin/entrypoint.sh"]

# --- Additional files to be placed in the same directory as the Dockerfile ---

# nginx.conf (for Nginx web server)
# server {
#     listen 80;
#     server_name localhost;
#
#     location / {
#         root /app/webserver;
#         index index.html;
#     }
#
#     error_page 500 502 503 504 /50x.html;
#     location = /50x.html {
#         root /usr/share/nginx/html;
#     }
# }

# index.html (for web server static content)
# <!DOCTYPE html>
# <html>
# <head>
#     <title>Web Server</title>
# </head>
# <body>
#     <h1>Hello from the Web Server!</h1>
#     <p>This is a static file served by Nginx.</p>
# </body>
# </html>

# app.py (for API server using Flask)
# from flask import Flask
#
# app = Flask(__name__)
#
# @app.route('/')
# def hello_world():
#     return 'Hello from the API Server!'
#
# @app.route('/data')
# def get_data():
#     # In a real app, this would query a database or another service
#     return {'message': 'This is some data from the API', 'version': '1.0'}
#
# if __name__ == '__main__':
#     app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=8080)

# entrypoint.sh (to start all services)
# #!/bin/bash
#
# # Start Nginx in the background
# echo "Starting Nginx..."
# service nginx start
#
# # Start Redis in the background
# echo "Starting Redis..."
# service redis-server start
#
# # Start the API server with Gunicorn in the background
# # Using exec to replace the shell, ensuring signals are passed correctly
# echo "Starting API server..."
# gunicorn --bind 0.0.0.0:8080 app:app --chdir /app/api_server &
#
# # Keep the container running
# # This is crucial. If this script exits, the container will stop.
# # A common pattern is to tail logs or run a sleep loop.
# # Tail Nginx access log, for example, to keep it alive
# echo "Container is running. Tailing /var/log/nginx/access.log (press Ctrl+C to stop)..."
# tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log &
#
# # Wait for all background processes to finish
# # This ensures that if any service exits, the container will eventually stop.
# # However, for development, you often want them to keep running.
# # The tail command above will keep it running.
# wait -n # wait for any background job to exit
# # Alternatively, if you want the container to run indefinitely even if one service crashes:
# # while true; do sleep 1000; done
#
# # If you want to explicitly see if processes are running:
# echo "Current running processes:"
# ps aux
